Q1: I organized my study routine around visual materials primarily - I'd print out diagrams, create color-coded notes, and spend hours with mind maps spread across my desk. At 27, I've learned what works for me, so I dedicated about 18 hours a week to studying, mostly in focused blocks. I preferred studying in the early mornings before my other responsibilities kicked in. My routine was built around creating visual summaries of everything I learned.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I tried to maintain some consistency, but being a bit older with more life responsibilities, I had to be flexible. Some weeks I'd front-load my studying, other weeks I'd have to cram everything into a few intense sessions. The key was making sure I hit my 18 hours somehow, even if the schedule shifted around.
Q3: Honestly, I logged into the LMS mainly when I needed to check on peer interactions or see if there were new materials to download. I found myself going in about six times to grab what I needed and then working offline. Sometimes fear of missing something important would prompt me to check in - that scared feeling of potentially falling behind would push me to log in and make sure I hadn't missed any announcements.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way my physical study materials did. I'd use it strategically - download videos, save PDFs, check in with peers - but then I'd retreat to my own study space. My routine was built around my printed notes and visual aids, not around being constantly connected to the platform.
Q5: The video content was incredibly valuable to me - I watched nearly two hours worth because visual explanations really click for me. I'd often watch a video once online, then download it or take detailed visual notes to study from later. The peer interaction features were also helpful; connecting with 39 different peers gave me different perspectives and helped me feel less alone in the struggle. Being able to see concepts demonstrated visually made complex ideas much more accessible.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion posts - I just didn't engage with that format at all. Writing out my thoughts in forums felt time-consuming and didn't match my learning style. I also minimized my time doing repetitive tasks on the LMS when I could achieve the same learning through my own visual study methods. If something felt like busywork rather than genuine learning, I'd skip it or find an alternative approach.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it gave me something tangible I could work with - a visual I could study, a concept I could map out, or a connection with a peer who could explain things differently. I needed to see the direct link between the activity and my understanding of the material. If I could picture how it would help me on the exam or in applying the knowledge, I was all in.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. With medium motivation and that underlying scared feeling I carry, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones were evaluated based on whether they'd genuinely help me learn better. Sometimes the optional peer discussions were more valuable than required readings, so I'd prioritize those instead.
Q9: Absolutely, this was the core of my studying. I'd download materials once and then work with them offline extensively - printing diagrams, creating visual summaries, using colored pens to annotate everything. I also used external resources like YouTube videos for visual explanations and borrowed textbooks from the library. My real learning happened at my desk surrounded by paper, not in front of the LMS.
Q10: Most of my actual studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours with my printed materials, creating elaborate mind maps and visual study guides. I'd review my handwritten notes on the bus, quiz myself using flashcards I'd made, and discuss concepts with study partners in person. The LMS would just show me logging in briefly, but I was putting in serious work offline that never got tracked.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or PDF once, download or take detailed notes from it, and then never need to access it online again. My task completion rate on the LMS looks low because I wasn't repeatedly engaging with materials there - I was engaging with my downloaded and printed versions instead. One thorough viewing was enough for me to create the visual materials I'd actually study from.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear crept in and made it harder to engage. When I felt scared about potentially failing or not understanding something, I'd sometimes freeze up instead of diving in. The peer support intervention helped during those times - connecting with others reminded me I wasn't alone. Competing responsibilities also made it tough; at 27, I have more on my plate than some younger students.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was exhausted, I'd skip logging in and just review my printed notes instead - it felt less overwhelming. Confusion sometimes kept me away too; if I didn't understand something, I'd feel scared to engage with more material until I'd sorted it out, usually through peer conversations or my own visual processing of the concepts offline.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd log in feeling scattered or anxious, scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. Those scared moments would hit and I'd be looking at the screen but not really processing. I'd recognize this was happening and force myself to step away, returning to my visual study methods where I felt more in control. Meaningful engagement required me to be in the right headspace.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts entirely - I knew they might be valuable but the format just didn't work for me and my anxiety around written contributions online was real. I also delayed some assignments when the fear got overwhelming, even though I knew they were important. My resilience usually kicked in eventually and I'd push through, but there were definitely avoidance patterns driven by that scared feeling.
Q16: Deadlines created a surge of activity for me, though not always visible in the LMS. I'd intensify my offline studying as deadlines approached, spending more time with my visual materials and less time anywhere else. The LMS might show me checking in more frequently near deadlines, but the real work was happening with my printed notes and study guides. Deadlines focused my energy, even when they also increased my fear.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me - when I got positive feedback on my exam scores and assignments, it helped counter that scared feeling and motivated me to keep going. Good feedback made me more willing to engage with challenging material because I had evidence I could succeed. Negative or unclear feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety, making me retreat into my comfortable offline study methods until I felt ready to try again.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation in mixed ways. Having clear modules helped me plan my visual study approach, but low attendance requirements meant I could focus on self-directed learning. The peer interaction opportunities were structured well enough that I could engage meaningfully with 39 peers. However, the structure didn't always accommodate my visual learning style, so I had to adapt materials myself.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier - visual resources are my strength. Having downloadable materials was crucial because it let me study in my preferred way. What made it harder was the emphasis on discussion posts, which didn't suit my learning style at all. Also, the task completion tracking didn't account for offline engagement, which sometimes made my progress look worse than it actually was.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it, or they might be like me - downloading everything to work with offline but not completing the tracked tasks. High activity could also mean someone is anxious and checking repeatedly without productive engagement. Or they might be supporting peers, which I did a lot of, without actually submitting their own work. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity looks modest - only six logins, low task completion - but I earned a Merit with strong exam and assignment scores. I was learning extensively offline with my visual methods, engaging deeply with downloaded materials and peer conversations that happened outside the tracked system. Low LMS activity can mask serious, effective studying happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing pretty accurately - when students log in, what they click on, how long they're connected. But they completely miss offline engagement, the quality of understanding, emotional state during learning, and alternative study methods. My logs would show minimal engagement when I was actually one of the more dedicated students - just not in ways the system could see.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until I understand it, not just clicking through content. For me, it's creating those visual maps, discussing concepts with peers until they click, and feeling confident enough to apply what I've learned. It's about depth of understanding and connection to the material, which can happen entirely offline. True engagement is internal and active, not just visible activity.
Q24: The LMS would miss almost everything important about my engagement - my 18 hours of weekly offline studying, my elaborate visual notes and mind maps, my in-person discussions with peers, and the emotional journey of pushing through fear to achieve. It wouldn't show my resilience in bouncing back from scared moments, or how peer support helped me persist. The data captures shadows of my learning, not the substance of it.
